title: Which Type of Learning Fits Which Problem?
concept: Recognizing whether a real-world problem has labeled answers, no labels, or a reward signal tells you whether it calls for supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the agent in reinforcement learning?

options.text: The thing taking actions and receiving rewards or penalties
A group discovered with no labels

exercises.explain: Right! The agent is whatever is taking actions and learning from rewards and penalties.

cards.title: Detective Work: Spot the Type
cards.body: You're handed a new AI problem and need to figure out, before writing a single line of code, which of the three types of learning it needs. Turns out there's a simple detective trick: ask what kind of feedback the data actually gives you.

cards.title: Three questions, three answers
cards.body: Ask: Does every example come with a correct answer? That's supervised. Is there no answer at all, just raw data? That's unsupervised. Does the system only get a reward or penalty after acting? That's reinforcement.

cards.title: Reading the map before the trip
cards.body: Picking the right type of learning is like reading a map before a road trip: check whether you have a labeled destination (supervised), an open area to explore with no set route (unsupervised), or a path you'll learn by taking wrong turns and adjusting (reinforcement).

cards.title: Three real problems, three answers
cards.body: A spam filter trained on emails already marked spam or not spam needs supervised learning. Grouping customers by shopping habits with no predefined categories needs unsupervised learning. A game-playing AI that only finds out if a move was good after seeing the score needs reinforcement learning.

cards.title: There's no single "best" type
cards.body: People sometimes assume one type of learning is the most advanced, or always the right choice. In reality, the correct type depends entirely on what kind of feedback the problem actually offers — not which one sounds fanciest.

cards.title: Match the clue to the type
cards.body: Two of the three telltale clues:

exercises.prompt: A hospital has thousands of X-rays, each already marked by a doctor as "healthy" or "shows a fracture." Which type of learning fits?
exercises.explain: Right! Every X-ray already has a correct label attached, which calls for supervised learning.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a problem gives no labeled answers and no reward signal, but just raw data to explore, unsupervised learning is the right fit.
exercises.explain: True! With no labels and no rewards, the only option left is finding patterns on your own — unsupervised learning.

exercises.sentence: To pick the right type of learning, first ask what kind of ___ the problem provides — labels, nothing, or rewards.
exercises.explain: The kind of feedback a problem offers — labels, no labels, or rewards — points to the right type of learning.

exercises.prompt: A grocery delivery app has no predefined shopper categories but wants to discover natural groupings in years of purchase data. Which type of learning fits best?

options.text: Unsupervised learning, since there's no answer key and it needs to find its own groupings
Supervised learning, since every purchase is labeled with a shopper type
Reinforcement learning, since the app is directly rewarded for each purchase

exercises.explain: Correct! With no predefined categories to check against, the app must discover its own groupings — that's unsupervised learning.

exercises.prompt: A game studio builds an AI opponent that only finds out whether a move was good by seeing whether it eventually won or lost the match — there's no list of "correct moves" and no pre-existing groups to sort players into. Which type of learning does this call for, and why?

options.text: Reinforcement learning, because the AI takes actions and only learns from a reward (winning) or penalty (losing) after the fact
Supervised learning, because every move comes labeled as correct or incorrect
Unsupervised learning, because the AI is just grouping similar matches together
None of the three — this problem doesn't fit any category

exercises.explain: Exactly! The AI only receives a win-or-lose signal after acting, with no labeled moves and no grouping task — that's the trial-and-error loop of reinforcement learning.
